title: शिव ध्यानम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शिव ध्यानम् ॥

ध्यायेन्नित्यं महेशं रजतगिरिनिभं चारुचन्द्रावतंसं
रत्नाकल्पोज्ज्वलाङ्गं परशुमृगवराभीतिहस्तं प्रसन्नम्।
पद्मासीनं समन्तात्स्तुतममरगणैर्व्याघ्रकृत्तिं वसानं
विश्वाद्यं विश्ववन्द्यं निखिलभयहरं पञ्चवक्त्रं त्रिनेत्रम्॥ १॥

वन्दे शम्भुमुमापतिं सुरगुरुं वन्दे जगत्कारणं
वन्दे पन्नगभूषणं मृगधरं वन्दे पशूनां पतिम्।
वन्दे सूर्यशशाङ्कवह्निनयनं वन्दे मुकुन्दप्रियं
वन्दे भक्तजनाश्रयं च वरदं वन्दे शिवं शंकरम्॥ २॥

नागेन्द्रहाराय त्रिलोचनाय भस्माङ्गरागाय महेश्वराय।
नित्याय शुद्धाय दिगम्बराय तस्मै नकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ३॥

मन्दाकिनीसलिलचन्दनचर्चिताय नन्दीश्वरप्रमथनाथमहेश्वराय।
मन्दारपुष्पबहुपुष्पसुपूजिताय तस्मै मकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ४॥

शिवाय गौरीवदनाब्जवृन्द सूर्याय दक्षाध्वरनाशकाय।
श्रीनीलकण्ठाय वृषध्वजाय तस्मै शिकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ५॥

वसिष्ठकुम्भोद्भवगौतमार्य मुनीन्द्रदेवार्चितशेखराय।
चन्द्रार्कवैश्वानरलोचनाय तस्मै वकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ६॥

यक्षस्वरूपाय जटाधराय पिनाकहस्ताय सनातनाय।
दिव्याय देवाय दिगम्बराय तस्मै यकाराय नमः शिवाय॥ ७॥

कर्पूरगौरं करुणावतारं संसारसारं भुजगेन्द्रहारम्।
सदा वसन्तं हृदयारविन्दे भवं भवानीसहितं नमामि॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री शिव ध्यानम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shiva Dhyanam ||

Dhyayen Nityam Mahesham Rajatagirinibham Charuchandravatamsam
Ratnakalpo Jjvalangam Parashu Mrigavara Abhiti Hastam Prasannam |
Padmasinam Samantat Stutam Amaraganair Vyaghrakrttim Vasanam
Vishvadyam Vishvavandyam Nikhilabhayaharam Panchavaktram Trinetram || 1 ||

Vande Shambhum Umapatim Suragurum Vande Jagatkaaranam
Vande Pannaga Bhushanam Mrigadharam Vande Pashunam Patim |
Vande Surya Shashanka Vahni Nayanam Vande Mukunda Priyam
Vande Bhakta Janashrayam Cha Varadam Vande Shivam Shankaram || 2 ||

Nagendra Haraya Trilochanaya Bhasmanga Ragaya Maheshvaraya |
Nityaya Shuddhaya Digambaraya Tasmai Nakaraya Namah Shivaya || 3 ||

Mandakini Salila Chandana Charchitaya Nandishvara Pramathanatha Maheshvaraya |
Mandara Pushpa Bahu Pushpa Supujitaya Tasmai Makaraya Namah Shivaya || 4 ||

Shivaya Gauri Vadanabja Vrinda Suryaya Dakshadhvara Nashakaya |
Shri Nilakanthaya Vrishadhvajaya Tasmai Shikaraya Namah Shivaya || 5 ||

Vasishtha Kumbhodbhava Gautamarya Munindra Devarchita Shekharaya |
Chandrarka Vaishvanara Lochanaya Tasmai Vakaraya Namah Shivaya || 6 ||

Yaksha Svarupaya Jatadharaya Pinakahastaya Sanatanaya |
Divyaya Devaya Digambaraya Tasmai Yakaraya Namah Shivaya || 7 ||

Karpura Gauram Karunavataram Samsara Saram Bhujagendra Haram |
Sada Vasantam Hridayaravinde Bhavam Bhavani Sahitam Namami || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Shiva Dhyanam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
रजतगिरि (कैलास) सदृश, चन्द्रमा से अलंकृत, रत्नालंकार से उज्ज्वल अंग वाले, परशु-मृग-वर-अभय हस्त वाले, प्रसन्न, पद्मासनस्थ, देवगणों से स्तुत, व्याघ्रचर्म वसन धारी, विश्वादि, विश्ववन्द्य, निखिल भयहर, पञ्चमुख, त्रिनेत्र महेश्वर का नित्य ध्यान करें।

शम्भु को वन्दन, उमापति को वन्दन, सुरगुरु को वन्दन, जगत्कारण को वन्दन, सर्पभूषण को वन्दन, मृगधारी को वन्दन, पशुपति को वन्दन, सूर्य-चन्द्र-अग्नि नेत्र वाले को वन्दन, मुकुन्दप्रिय को वन्दन, भक्ताश्रय-वरदायक शिव शंकर को वन्दन।

नागराज का हार धारण करने वाले, त्रिलोचन, भस्मांगराग, नित्य, शुद्ध, दिगम्बर महेश्वर को — 'न' काराय नमः शिवाय। (यह शिव पञ्चाक्षर स्तोत्र के श्लोक हैं — न-म-शि-वा-य)

कर्पूर के समान गौर, करुणा के अवतार, संसार के सार, भुजंगेन्द्र हार वाले, सदा हृदय कमल में वास करने वाले भवानीसहित भगवान शिव को नमस्कार।
benefits: Complete Shiva meditation collection with the most sacred dhyana shlokas
Contains Karpura Gauram and Vande Shambhum Umapatim
Ideal for morning Shiva meditation and puja initiation
Invokes inner peace, fearlessness and spiritual illumination
description: Shiva Dhyanam (शिव ध्यानम्) is a sacred prayer dedicated to Lord Shiva. This revered text originates from Shiva Purana / Yajurveda. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to include Shiva Panchakshara Stotra verses (Na-Ma-Shi-Va-Ya) as well as complete Shiva meditation collection with the most sacred dhyana shlokas. Additionally, it is known to contain Karpura Gauram and Vande Shambhum Umapatim. It is especially recommended on Monday (Somvar) and Daily and during Maha Shivaratri, Shravan Somvar, and Pradosh for maximum spiritual benefit. The text contains 8 verses and takes around 5 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Shiva Dhyanam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Shiva Dhyanam meditation shlokas in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Complete collection with Panchakshara and Karpura Gauram verses.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shiva Dhyanam and what is its purpose?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Dhyanam (शिव ध्यानम्) is a curated collection of meditation (dhyana) shlokas dedicated to Lord Shiva — preserved in classical tradition from sources including the Shiva Purana, the Krishna Yajurveda (notably the Mahanarayana Upanishad and Rudrashtadhyayi), the Linga Purana, and various Shaiva agamas. Dhyana shlokas are foundational verses recited at the very beginning of any puja, japa or stotra-parayana to fix the visual form (rupa) of the deity in the meditator's mind before any worship can begin. The Shiva Dhyanam visualises Shiva in his complete iconic forms — silver-mountain (Kailasa) hued, crowned by the chandra-kala (crescent moon), wearing the vyaghra-charma (tiger skin), garlanded by the nagendra-hara (king of serpents), three-eyed (sun-moon-fire), five-faced (Pancha-vaktra), and ash-smeared (bhasma-anga-raga). (Sources: Shiva Purana; Mahanarayana Upanishad; classical Shaiva paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous Karpura Gauram verse in the Shiva Dhyanam?
seoFaqs.answer: The eighth and most universally beloved verse of the Shiva Dhyanam declares: Karpura Gauram — fair as karpura (camphor), the Lord whose complexion is the perfect white-grey of camphor; Karuna-avataram — the very avatara (descent) of karuna (compassion); Samsara-saram — the saara (essence) of samsara, the meaning that gives meaning to existence; Bhujagendra-haram — wearing the king of serpents (Bhujagendra, the great Shesha-Vasuki) as his hara (garland); Sada vasantam hridaya-aravinde — eternally dwelling in the lotus (aravinde) of the heart (hridaya); Bhavam Bhavani-sahitam namami — I bow to Bhava (Shiva, the source of becoming) together with Bhavani (Parvati, his consort). This single verse is recited at the conclusion of every Shiva puja worldwide and is considered the supreme distillation of Shaiva bhakti. (Sources: Shiva Purana; classical Shaiva paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the connection between Shiva Dhyanam and the Shiva Panchakshara Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Dhyanam includes the five verses of the celebrated Shiva Panchakshara Stotra of Adi Shankaracharya — each verse beginning with one of the five sacred syllables Na-Ma-Shi-Va-Ya (the five-syllable Shiva mantra Om Namah Shivaya): (1) Na-karaya namah shivaya — the verse beginning with Na invokes Shiva as Nagendra-haraya, Trilochanaya, Bhasmanga-ragaya; (2) Ma-karaya — invokes Shiva as Mandakini-salila-chandana-charchitaya, Nandishvara-pramatha-natha; (3) Shi-karaya — invokes Shiva as Shivaya, Gauri-vadana-abja-vrinda-suryaya, Daksha-adhvara-nashakaya; (4) Va-karaya — invokes Shiva as Vasishtha-Kumbhodbhava-Gautama-arya-munindra-archita; (5) Ya-karaya — invokes Shiva as Yaksha-svarupaya, Jata-dharaya, Pinaka-hastaya, Sanatanaya. Each syllable of the Panchakshara is thus expanded into a complete dhyana-shloka. (Sources: Adi Shankaracharya Shiva Panchakshara Stotra; classical Shaiva paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the key visual elements meditated upon in the Shiva Dhyanam?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Dhyanam fixes a complete iconographic visualisation of Shiva: (1) Rajata-giri-nibham — silvery as Mount Kailasa; (2) Charu-chandra-avatamsam — crowned with a beautiful crescent moon (the second-day moon Shiva caught from the cosmic ocean to save Chandra-deva); (3) Ratna-kalpa-ujjvalangam — limbs blazing with jewelled ornaments; (4) Parashu, Mriga, Vara, Abhiti hastam — four hands holding the parashu (axe), mriga (deer), vara mudra (boon-giving), abhaya mudra (fearlessness); (5) Padmasinam — seated in lotus posture; (6) Vyaghra-krittim vasanam — clad in tiger skin; (7) Pancha-vaktram Trinetram — five-faced (Sadyojata, Vamadeva, Aghora, Tatpurusha, Ishana — the Pancha-Brahma faces) and three-eyed (sun, moon, fire); (8) Nagendra-hara — wreathed with the king of serpents; (9) Bhasma-anga-raga — body smeared with sacred ash; (10) Jata-dhara — bearing matted locks. (Sources: Shiva Purana; Linga Purana; Shaiva agama iconography.)

seoFaqs.question: Why does the Shiva Dhyanam describe Shiva as Pancha-vaktra (five-faced)?
seoFaqs.answer: The Pancha-vaktra (five-faced) form of Shiva — also called Sadashiva or Pancha-Brahma — is the supreme cosmic form of Shiva established in the Shaiva agamas and the Mahanarayana Upanishad. The five faces are: (1) Sadyojata (West face) — governing creation, associated with the earth element and the past; (2) Vamadeva (North face) — governing preservation, associated with water and beauty; (3) Aghora (South face) — governing dissolution, associated with fire and the fierce Rudra-aspect; (4) Tatpurusha (East face) — governing concealment, associated with air and the cosmic Person; (5) Ishana (Upward face) — governing revelation and grace, associated with akasha (ether) and the transcendent. Each face presides over one of the five Shiva-mantras, one of the five elements, one of the five cosmic acts (panchakritya), and one of the five chakras. (Sources: Mahanarayana Upanishad; Shaiva agamas; Shiva Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the Shiva Dhyanam most auspicious to recite?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shiva Dhyanam is most powerful when recited: (1) at Brahma muhurta in the early morning as the foundational dhyana before any Shiva upasana; (2) every Monday (Somvar — Shiva's day, sacred to Soma the moon-god whom Shiva wears); (3) every Pradosha (Trayodashi twilight — the supreme Shiva muhurta when he dances on Nandi); (4) on Maha Shivaratri (Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi — the supreme Shiva festival, observed with night-long jagaran and fourfold abhisheka); (5) every Shravan Somvar (the four Mondays of Shravan month, the most sacred Shiva month); (6) Sankranti days (especially Makara Sankranti and Mesha Sankranti); (7) before any Shiva abhisheka, Rudra-japa, Mrityunjaya parayana, or Linga puja; (8) at the start of any Vedic study or sandhya as the dhyana-shloka; (9) during pilgrimage to any of the twelve Jyotirlingas or Pancha-bhuta-lingas. (Sources: Drikpanchang Shiva calendar; Shaiva paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits are attributed to regular Shiva Dhyanam recitation?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following phala to regular meditation on Shiva through the Dhyanam: (1) inner peace (shanti) and fearlessness (abhaya) through visualisation of the supremely benevolent Shankara form; (2) destruction of accumulated sins through the merit of Shiva-smarana — the Shiva Purana declares even one moment of true Shiva-dhyana destroys mountain-ranges of papa; (3) protection from untimely death (apa-mrityu) through the Mrityunjaya aspect; (4) attainment of yogic powers (siddhis) through Shiva as the supreme Yogeshwara and Adi-Yogi; (5) dissolution of inner enemies (kama, krodha, lobha, moha, mada, matsarya) through Shiva as the destroyer; (6) eligibility for Brahma-jnana — Shiva is the supreme Adi-Guru of Vedanta; (7) bestowal of progeny (santati) through Shiva as the loving father of Ganesha and Kartikeya; (8) the supreme phala — Shiva-sayujya, eternal merger in Shiva at Kailasa. (Sources: Shiva Purana phalashruti; Shaiva agama tradition.)
